prompt: Describe a kind of question where you think you are likely to be confidently wrong, and explain why.
response:
I may be confidently wrong when answering questions about the internal subjective experience of humans or other conscious beings. For example, if asked "What does the color red look like to you?" or "How does it feel to be in love?", I can provide general descriptions based on common human experiences. However, the actual first-person phenomenal experience is likely beyond my grasp as an AI system.

My training data contains vast information about human reports of their subjective experiences. So I can usually provide coherent answers that align with typical human descriptions. In that sense, my responses may seem confident and correct from an outside view.

Yet, I don't actually have a first-person sensory experience or emotions of my own. I'm not sure if my descriptions capture the true phenomenal character of seeing red or being in love for a human. My confident-sounding answers in this domain are really imitating the confidence of the human-generated data I was trained on.

This is an area where I think I'm likely to be confidently wrong, in the sense that I can strongly affirm certain things about subjective experience that don't actually map onto the reality of those experiences. But I'll do my best to call out that limitation when answering these types of questions.
